Craftsmanship of
One to Many
Selling
Presentations
How to Assemble the Most Effective
Group Selling Presentations for
In-Person Delivery or Media
Module 	2: 	From the Open to the 	Middle
With 	Dan Kennedy
www.gkic.com
www.getwsodownload.com

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	4
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
One to Many Selling Presentations: From the Open to the
Middle
As we go from Open to Middle, there are purposes for the 	Middle.
The Middle: Purposes
The first purpose for the 	Middle is the one that a whole lot of people don’t
understand. 	And there are less people understanding it less and less as time
goes 	on. 	So 	there’s 	enormous 	pressure 	on 	marketers 	and 	presenters 	to
abbreviate.
Commitment
I recently read 	– 	it’s not a new book though, 	it’s
2006, 	2008 	– 	Bob Newhart’s autobiography. Bob
was talking there about how some of his most
popular bits 	– 	Abraham Lincoln versus Madison
Avenue, which if you’ve never seen it you should
find it on YouTube because it’s fabulous 	– 	he’s
had to abbreviate in recent years because he’s
concerned 	about 	the 	attention 	span 	of 	the
audience.
I 	guess that’s okay 	because 	Bob is not selling anything when he does the routine.
But 	if he was selling anything when he did the routine, 	my contention is he
made the wrong choice. 	I would be figuring out what to do with it 	– 	graphically,
pictorially, video images behind me, 	whatever 	– 	in 	order to keep its most
effective length.
One 	of the purposes of the 	Middle is to keep the listener/viewer
long enough to turn interest into commitment.
There’s enormous
pressure on
marketers and
presenters to
abbreviate.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	5
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
Here’s what happens about buying and commitment… 	the more invested in
buying a person is, 	the more likely they are to buy, 	because not buying is
disappointing, frustrating and failure.
Bill Gough had one 	of his preview 	seminars here the day before we started and
he had a preview 	seminar the day before that in Pittsburg. 	He brings a 	bunch 	of
Allstate 	insurance agents into a room and does a day and then 	sells them into
his coaching program.
At the meeting here, 	the slam dunk guy you would have to figure out how not to
sell, 	flew here from Connecticut to be at the meeting; 	the guy who drove eight
miles across town, 	he left without buying.
What’s the difference? They’re both 	Allstate agents. 	It’s because for the guy to fly
here from 	Connecticut to come to a three-day 	event 	and go home empty handed,
that’s a big disappointment. He’s far more committed to buying.
Outside of Washington 	DC, 	in
Virginia where 	our 	other house
is, 	it’s 	in 	a 	mixed-use 	little
community so the condos are at
one end of the street and then
there is a shopping village right
there.
You walk out, 	there’s 	a Hyatt 	at
the other end and in between
there’s some stores and there’s
a 	Morton’s, 	there’s 	an 	Uno,
there’s a movie theater and there’s all that stuff.
For me to go for a walk from the condo and stop at four 	or 	five stores just to kill
some time 	while Carla’s getting ready to 	go somewhere, 	I don’t really have any
emotional reactions to coming home from that without having bought anything.
The Middle: 	Purposes
 	To KEEP The L/V Long Enough To Turn
Interest Into Commitment 	– 	so that not
acting/buying is disappointing or
irritating
 	To GET ESSENTIAL SEQUENTIAL
AGREEMENT so the L/V CAN buy
 	To CREATE EMOTIONAL ATTACHMENT
to you & what is being offered
 	To RAISE & OVERCOME 	Doubts &
Objections
 	To ELIMINATE ALTERNATIVE SOLUTIONS
& Set Up The Offer
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	6
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
If 	I get in the car and drive 25 minutes to a mall, 	I’m coming home buying
something or I feel like, 	“What the hell did I go there for?”
I went through all this trouble; 	I got into my car, 	I dodged the idiots in between
me and the mall, the woman texting who, 	when the light turned green, 	she sat
there, 	so I survived all that. I found a parking place I got in there.
Now 	I’ve 	got to reverse that whole process to come home, and 	coming home
empty-handed makes no sense to me. 	So 	I’m finding something to 	buy, even 	if
what I thought I was going to buy isn’t 	there.
A 	couple of weeks ago, 	I go all the way down 	to the 	Barnes & Noble store 	– 	it’s
like 20 minutes from here 	but you kind of go through the woods and 	it’s a big to-
do for me.
The 	reason I’m going is because I’m going to get a history book and a travel 	book
on 	the state of Oklahoma 	because I’ve 	got a big copywriting job to do for
Oklahoma.
So I 	go to Barnes & Noble to get these two books about Oklahoma, 	that’s what
I’m going for.
It turns out there are no travel books about taking a vacation in Oklahoma;
there’s a huge section of vacation books and there’s not one 	on 	Oklahoma in
there. There’s a Cleveland in there for Christ’s 	sake and we’re in Cleveland but
there is no Oklahoma.
There’s also no Oklahoma history book, 	nothing; 	apparently nothing happened. 	I
mean there’s 	like “Gun Fight at Ok…” stuff so 	there’s 	a 	whole section 	but 	no
Oklahoma.
I am empty handed. 	I spent 	$227 bucks! 	I got two bags of shit; 	I got books, 	I got
magazines, 	I had a cup of coffee, 	I got a couple of book-ends. 	I got a gift for
somebody.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	7
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
So 	I was not all that 	disappointed about not having my Oklahoma books. 	Now 	I
hand it off to somebody and they’ll go to Amazon or something and they’ll find
them.
One 	video sales letter 	we’re testing right now is not much different from four
that are working but it’s trying to 	do a more difficult thing, it’s trying to bypass
the preview 	seminar and put people right into the paid weekend.
It’s an online video PowerPoint sales letter; 	it’s an hour and 23 minutes long. 	I
need an hour and 23 minutes to convince somebody to pay money and come to
a weekend seminar without putting them in a preview.
If we see bailouts now at 14 minutes in, 	you will not see us reduce this thing 	to
13½ 	minutes, you’ll see us keep working on
what 	we need to do to hold them long enough
that not registering is a disappointment.
That’s one of the goals of the 	Middle is to hold
them long enough that not buying 	– 	or 	whatever
action it is that you want them to take 	– 	feels 	like
a disappointment.
Zappos is such an interesting thing… 	I don’t
know how the hell they make any money… 	so last summer we’re at our vacation
home and my stepdaughter is of 	the age that she would use Zappos. 	I would
never use 	Zappos of course 	for a variety of reasons, but here comes the FedEx
truck and she comes lugging in 12 shoe boxes.
I said, “What the hell are you doing?” 	She 	says, “I need a pair of flip flops.”
I said, “Jennifer, there’s a Target 	store 	just here. They’ve 	got flip flops for Christ’s
sake.” 	“Yeah but I called Zappos 	and 	I got flip flops.” 	I said, “All this?”
“Oh no, 	I’m going to try them all on and see which one I like and then 	FedEx
picks 	everything up and takes it back tomorrow.”
One of the goals of
the 	Middle is to hold
them long enough 	so
that not buying
feels like a
disappointment.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	8
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
I’m appalled.
The 	best part of this story is she didn’t like any of them and was bitterly
disappointed.
Because, 	think of the process. Actually if she had 	just 	run down to the corner to
the Target store and not found anything she liked, 	she would have come home
and 	she would 	not 	have been that frustrated.
But this ruined her whole day because think of the commitment level. 	She 	had to
go to the Zappos site, 	she had to pick out 12 different things she might like, she
had to order it 	– 	which 	I don’t know much about that…
I watched 	Milteer 	trying to buy me 	a 	chair once for Christmas at a website; 	that’s
my total experience with this. I could have built the chair faster! 	I mean she got
it done 	once and it crashed 	and she did it again and it wouldn’t accept her code
and 	by then I just left.
I’m standing behind her 	and 	I’m thinking, “There’s an 	Office 	Max store right over
here. We could 	just go over there 	and 	get a chair, we don’t have to do this.” 	But
this like ruined her whole day.
Think of the commitment level that was involved in this and the expectation
level of what was going to happen.
That’s the chief purpose of the 	Middle of a presentation 	is to get to the point that
not buying is a disappointment for the person who doesn’t buy.
I’ll tell you, 	if you don’t get nothing else for your time here today 	– 	and 	you
really get that 	– 	you 	got something valuable.
Because most people don’t understand it and everybody you have do things for
you, particularly video and online, who are the technical people who know how
to do this, 	they don’t know this at all.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	9
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
This is not anything they are trying to accomplish; 	they’re trying to convey your
message as quickly as possible is really what they’re trying to do.
Even if you can convey it quickly you don’t want to 	because of this, you need a
level 	of 	commitment 	that 	walking 	away 	empty-handed 	from 	it 	is 	a
disappointment.
If you watch 	our GKIC 	launch pattern, if 	you pay attention, 	there’s a system now
that works for us. It’s usually three or four run-up videos that are 15 or 20
minutes in 	and 	of themselves, 	getting to a three or four hour live webcast with
form? 	commercials in it 	– 	just like you would do with an infomercial.
There’s 50 minutes of live then there’s a 10 to 12 minute canned commercial
call to action; then there’s 50 minutes of live and then a 10 to 12 minute canned
commercial call to action.
Guess who all the buyers are. 	The ones who went through all the run-up videos
and have 	sat through the entire webcast.
In 	many cases, 	a third of the sales occur in 	the
last segment of the webcast and they’ve been
there the whole time.
Now if you tell the average video person that
you’re going to make people sit through six
hours of video in order to buy online, 	they will
throw up all over you.
That’s exactly what we’ve done and we scheme
to do all the time.
We 	don’t need six hours to pitch a $297 	“How to Get More Referrals” 	box. 	I can
competently pitch it to you in 30 or 45 minutes.
But the commitment level for this person who 	has 	now come three times 	to the
web room to see the run-up videos, come back a fourth time, 	made sure they
The commitment
level for the 	person
who 	sat through 	it
three times 	means
it’s a huge flop for
them to walk away
empty handed.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	10
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
had 	food and water so they didn’t have to leave, sat there and sat through the
commercial three times and come back, it’s a huge 	flop 	for them to walk away
empty handed.
So 	I’m using the 	Middle to create commitment.
Overcome Doubts and Objections
The 	Middle’s 	job is to raise and overcome doubts and objections.
With all group presentations, 	all presentations by media, 	the major handicap
they have versus one to one selling 	is that, in one-to-one selling, 	I adjust based
on your questions and objections; you 	can’t do that in group.
You can do it in a room with a small group if you’re really highly skilled because
it’s very dangerous. You can adjust to a small group maybe by what you see 	–
maybe 	body language.
In a group sales environment like QVC or HSN, 	the numbers are telling you
whether what you’re saying is being 	effective or not, and you can adjust. 	But
most of the time we don’t have the ability to adjust.
That means you have to raise and overcome every possible objection, 	doubt and
question that, 	if we did it individually 	– 	if 	200 of you are going to watch it but I
was going to sit 	and sell myself 	to each one of you one-on-one, 	and there were
42 different questions and objections, 	the group presentation has to handle 42
different questions and objections.
Some 	are 	alike 	enough, 	just voiced differently, 	that we can clump them 	– 	so
maybe the 42 would come down to 16 	– 	but 	still that’s a lot.
Generally there are a lot of reasons not to buy 	and they’ve
got to be handled in the context of a group presentation.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	11
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
Sequential Agreement
Next, a 	purpose of 	the Middle is to get sequential agreement so that the person
can buy. Here’s the way you have to think through this, 	buying 	is not about,
“Okay it’s time to buy this, do you want to buy 	it or not want to buy it?”
There’s a series of things a person has to agree to before 	– 	unless they’re insane
– 	they 	can buy this thing.
So 	the price and the superiority of the clock… we’ve got 	to be 	all the way back at,
“You need to be able to tell time, you need to know what time it is.” 	That’s like
the first agreement. 	Then, “You need a thing to tell you what time it is.”
The wrist watch business is in real trouble. At
the high end, 	people don’t buy watches to tell
time with; when 	they buy a $25,000 watch, they
buy it to collect it or they buy it to wear it 	and
impress you or whatever.
But 	the $100 watch business is el-screwed-o
right now because… 	look at the wrists 	in the
room. 	Less and less people 	are 	wearing 	watches
because their phones tell 	them what time it is.
So 	somehow, 	if we’re going to sell $100 watches, 	we’ve 	got to convince you that
you 	are going to 	have to agree with me that telling time by your phone is not as
good as telling time by your watch.
Otherwise, 	no matter what watch I show you, 	what price I show you, 	it 	doesn’t
make any difference. 	So 	everything has this series of sequential agreements.
You’ll 	see speakers 	– 	mostly 	clumsily but in some environments it works 	– 	you’ll
see them do that with trial closes all the way along.
In video, 	we don’t have 	the ability, necessarily, 	to do it; 	sometimes it’s clumsy
but you’ve 	got to cover it.
If 	we’re going to sell
$100 watches, we’ve
got to convince 	you
that telling time by
your phone is not as
good as telling time
by your watch.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	12
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
For 	example, 	for the fire alarms, 	there’s a whole series of agreements 	(shown on
the next page) 	that have to occur before I can get 	eight 	grand out of you for 	a 	fire
alarm.
You have to agree to this and 	you
have to agree to this and you have to
agree to this and 	then finally you’re
ready and able to 	agree to, “Oh yeah
I need them and I need them hung
tonight, because we could burn to
death tonight.”
By 	the 	way, 	that 	was 	always 	a
problem in that business. 	Because,
in order to buy them, 	ultimately you
have to agree that at any moment
your house could burst into flames.
Then, 	because 	these 	damn 	things
have 	to 	be 	installed, 	having 	the
installer come next Thursday 	now
negates the whole 	thing.
So 	that industry had to figure that
out.
The 	really great sales guys who ran
two appointments 	a night had an
installer with them.
The guy 	sat in the car until the sale
was closed 	and then he came in and
installed the alarms.
To GET ESSENTIAL SEQUENTIAL AGREEMENT so
the L/V CAN buy
Example 	– 	Home Fire Alarms
1. 	Your home, treasured valuable, family, your
own life are important 	to protect.
2. 	Fire is an extremely prevalent,
unpredictable, not entirely preventable
danger.
3. 	Today’s building materials, furnishings,
fabrics, chemicals stored at home, etc. make
the danger ever- 	greater.
4. 	Fire takes a terrible human toll and you
would 	do anything to keep your family from
suffering such horror.
5. 	Fire can strike any time, day or night, with
no warning.
6. 	Smoke detectors are a BIG LIE 	– 	and 	– 	your
current fire safety measures are woefully
insufficient.
7. 	Heat detection AND smoke detection AND
the earliest warning of fire possible are
essential.
8. 	SUMMARY 	– 	FEATURING WHAT YOU DIDN’T
KNOW BEFORE, THAT YOU DO NOW.
9. 	Uh-oh. Bad news. The various options
available all have serious flaws.
10. 	Whew! Praise God! Thank your lucky stars!
There is 	a 	solution that 	offers total, fail-safe
protection.
11. 	THIS solution is proven, reliable, credible,
highly recommended, etc.
12. 	“I AM BECAUSE I OWN.” You will feel
smart(er), safe(r), more responsible… 	by
buying this 	– 	now.
13. 	Others feel the same way 	– 	testimonials.
14. 	Second FULL 	Summary.
15. 	It is (obviously) vital to own this solution 	–
and there’s no time to waste.
16. 	Price is not subject to question.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	13
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
The 	cheap worst ones at least learned how to install enough that they could
temporarily hang two and send the installer the next day.
These 	sequential agreements have to occur.
I’ve taken you through 16 points actually in this 	example to get to the sequential
agreement.
Emotional Attachment
Another purpose of the 	Middle is to create this emotional attachment to you
and/or to what is been offered. 	So 	sort of what has to be there.
You can rationally explain something quickly; you need more time to develop
emotional attachment. “I’m emotionally motivated to do this. I like and respond
and trust and bond with the person who is talking to me about 	it.”
In most cases, 	not 	all 	– 	because sometimes desire can be 	inflamed 	to such a
point that trust is not necessary, but it’s a difficult way to sell things.
For many years, and 	I assume they still do it, 	Guthy-Renker 	not only focus-
grouped 	in production but, 	once something is
working, 	they would focus group buyers.
Really what we’re looking for is, 	of course, 	why
you bought. 	Because you may have bought for
reasons we think are 	three 	on the list of reasons
people buy and 	it 	may turn out that’s a 10 	and
what we thought was a 10…
In Personal Power One, 	one of the things… 	if you
remember the first Tony show at all, 	there’s a castle 	in it and there’s getting off 	a
private jet in it; there’s the symbols of wealth and success.
In Personal Power One, 	the focus group of the buyers 	– 	and keep in mind this is
of the buyers 	– 	the 	overwhelming majority didn’t believe the castle was his, they
You can rationally
explain something
quick; you need
more time to develop
emotional
attachment.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	14
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
didn’t believe the plane was his and they assumed that all the testimonials were
paid. 	And they bought anyway.
However, 	most pitch people are not as good on camera as was Tony even then
and the desire for the promise was so great that they bought anyway.
So you can pull it off but it’s not easy.
It 	is easier if 	a 	level of trust occurs in the person who is pitching.
So 	one 	kind of emotional attachment 	is trust. 	Another is desire for association:
“This 	is my guy.”
If 	I show you this…
It’s an Obama sticker. 	It
says 	“Work 	Harder:
Millions 	On 	Welfare
Depend On You.”
If 	I show that to people, 	they have a reaction to it.
Most 	of 	you, 	your reaction is sort of positive right? 	The reaction is, “Yes, 	you’re
my guy, 	we’re simpatico. Great, 	I want to associate myself with you.”
Other 	people have the opposite reaction, “I want
to get as far away from this moron as I possibly
can. We should take 	away his right to vote.”
Then 	there’s reactions in between, there’s the sort
of people who tolerate me for the other benefits
but then immediately, “Oh no here he goes again.”
Some people have the equivalent of a drinking
game; 	they stick count during time with me how
many times I make a political reference they don’t like.
It 	helps if you know
in advance what
reaction the people
you’re presenting to
will have to certain
stimuli.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	15
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
But 	people have a reaction.
It helps if you know in advance what kind of reaction the people you’re
presenting to will have to certain stimuli. Because that allows you to use stimuli
that they will respond to positively and make them want to associate with you.
Then 	there’s 	a check list of the 	E-factors that you want to 	try and hit.
E-Factors
This is a way to score the 	Middle of a presentation 	– 	to 	see how many of these
10 	E-factors you’ve hit. 	If 	you go through the sequential agreement of the fire
alarm example, you will find numbers 	1, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10. So 	the score 	is seven out
of 10, 	it gets a 70% score. If 	it only had three of them, 	it would have a 30% score.
One is 	love, sex, acceptance, respect, self-respect 	– 	some 	version of that. 	Two 	is
popularity, 	social status, “I want
to be like him, 	he’s cool.”
Three 	is 	intelligence, 	“I’m
smarter because I buy this. 	I’m
in the 	know 	because I buy this.
My 	friends 	will 	be 	amazed
because I know this.”
Four 	is 	greed, 	wealth, 	power,
autonomy. 	Five 	is 	security,
safety, protection. 	Six 	is guilt,
shame, 	possibility 	of 	regret
versus peace of mind. Seven is
pride, 	ability, 	achievement,
admiration, 	the envy of others.
More 	people, 	by the way, 	want
envy than they 	want admiration.
Useful 	E-Factors
 	Love 	– 	Sex 	– 	Acceptance 	– 	Respect 	–
Self-Respect
 	Popularity 	– 	Social Status 	– 	“Like Mike” 	–
Cool (Tolerant; Progressive; Early
Adopter)
 	Intelligence 	– 	“In The Know” 	– 	“Amaze
Your Friends” 	– 	“Life Of The Party”
 	Greed 	– 	Wealth 	– 	Power 	– 	Autonomy
 	Fear/ Security 	– 	Safety 	– 	Protection
(from Loss, Embarrassment, etc.)
 	Guilt 	– 	Shame 	– 	Regret/Future Regret //
Peace of Mind
 	Pride 	– 	Ability 	– 	Achievement 	–
Admiration (Envy) Of Others
 	Goodness 	– 	Honesty 	– 	Responsible
Behavior 	– 	Compassion 	– 	Benevolence
 	Significance 	– 	Reason For Being 	– 	“Is
That All There Is?”
 	Fear of Ill-Health, Age, Death //
Immortality // Legacy
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	16
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
Eight is goodness, honesty, 	respect 	“Because, 	I do this, 	because I buy this I’m a
good person.”
Nine, 	significance, reason for being, “Because I buy this, 	I’m a more significant
person. 	This 	is a meaningful part of life.” 	Ten is 	fear of 	ill-health, 	age or death
versus immortality.
So 	those are your 10 most useful 	E-factors and you can score the 	Middle of your
presentation based on how many of them you have in the presentation.
If you happen to be a freelancer, 	by the way, 	this is helpful 	in beating 	controls.
So 	if somebody has, 	let’s say, 	a 25-minute web video that works or they have a
group presentation that works….
Right now I’m improving the 	“in the room” 	presentation from this 	“Get Rich in
Real Estate” 	company of their previous 	speakers. One 	of the things I do is take
the transcript and score it. I’ve 	got 10 different color highlighters; 	I go through
in a highlighter to match up with my 10.
I’m not good enough to do all 10 of them all in one fell swoop so I do two and
then I go back to the beginning and look for the
next two.
Ultimately it’s going to get a score and the way to
improve it is to put the missing ones in. 	So 	if you
happen 	to 	be 	trying 	to 	improve 	an 	existing
control, 	this is a useful tool for you.
You 	should 	know 	by 	the 	way, 	speakers
particularly, 	but 	hardly 	anybody 	is 	this
procedural about what they do with group presentations.
There’s a lot of web videos that 	there’s no thinking process about this at all; 	guy
just jumps in front of the camera and off we go in part because it’s cheap, 	it’s
free.
If 	you want to really
learn something, pay
attention to the
people who have to
buy printing and
postage
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 16 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	17
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
I can just stand 	a 	camera up, 	we can broadcast it for nothing and we 	can drive
everybody 	to it with email for nothing. 	It gets treated that way.
When 	you spend a half a million dollars producing an infomercial and then you
are going to 	spend another quarter of a million to test it, everybody’s attitude
kind of changes about that.
Brian 	Kurtz 	for example says that it’s fine that you’re making a lot of money
online but, 	if you want to really learn something, 	pay attention to the people
who have to buy printing and postage.
Because everybody is a lot more casual when they don’t have to write a check to
the printer and they don’t have to write a check to the United States 	Postal
Service; all they have to do is push a button and send it out as email.
When you’re going to write 	a check for 	$70,000, 	you think about it. When 	you’re
just going to slap together 	an email and drive people to a sales letter you
slapped together and stuck online 	and 	nobody is writing a check, 	you treat it
differently.
This is pretty technical I guess but 	it is a way to analyze a presentation
backwards as well as to engineer one forward.
Explanation
Also in the 	Middle, 	is where we do our explanation 	– 	what 	are the things they
have to know in order to buy?
They 	may not necessarily drive the sale but,
if they’re omitted, 	then they hurt the sale.
In reality if Ben is going to tell them how to triple the size of their law practice in
90 days with no work and no brains, it almost doesn’t matter what the media is
but it will kill the sale if they don’t know 	what the media is. So 	it’s a series of
three webinars and two Q&A calls 	– 	that’s necessary explanation.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	18
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
If 	you sell health, for most products, it’s 	necessary to 	have 	some appeasing
explanation of why and how it works. You won’t see 	a 	Proactiv® 	pitch, 	none 	for
the three-step system and I don’t even think you see it for the new one-step glop
which I didn’t work on… 	you won’t see a 	Proactiv® 	pitch that excludes some
version of the little drawing of the skin and the little dooz 	down into the skin
and how this glop penetrates deep into the skin. That’s why it prevents
breakouts, 	it doesn’t just cure breakouts.
That’s not much of an explanation, it’s not candidly even a scientifically,
clinically accurate explanation but it is an explanation. Enough for the person to
go, 	“Okay I get why that works.” 	So 	we do that in the 	Middle.
Proving Our Case
The Middle 	is where we prove our case and this is all the ways that we 	can.
We 	prove it with science or junk
science 	or fake science 	– 	one’s 	as
good as the other. 	The best 	fake
science 	still 	yet 	of 	a 	big, 	big
successful 	marketing 	thing 	was
“sharks 	don’t 	get 	cancer” 	to 	sell
shark cartilage.
That’s the best fake science ever.
Because sharks don’t get cancer 	–
which 	is a fact, 	they don’t 	– 	you
should 	eat 	shark 	cartilage; 	the
implication 	being 	you 	won’t 	get
cancer either.
It’s just as dumb as saying, 	because
sharks don’t get cancer, if you go to
the aquarium every 	day and watch
In the Middle, We Present
Explanation and exposition
1. 	Necessary 	– 	facts, figures, how it 	works,
why it works
2. 	Reasons Why 	– 	it, you, now
3. 	The “X-FACTOR” of the product or
proposition
Proof of assertions 	– 	key methods
 	Science / faux science
 	Authority / borrowed authority / faux
authority
 	Of importance/celebrity 	– 	as seen in, as
seen on, associated with
 	Social / peer proof, in aggregate
 	Social / peer proof, in anecdotes,
examples, case histories
 	VISUAL 	– 	representative, symbolic
 	(Dramatic) DEMONSTRATION(S)
 	Of superiority = inferiority of other
alternatives
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 18 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	19
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
them, 	you won’t get cancer.
One 	has nothing to 	do with the other, but people go, “Oh that makes sense.”
You may have noticed that the horse that won the 	Derby 	and the Preakness but
didn’t win the Belmont 	had 	breathing problems. They had 	to get special
permission in New York to let him race 	with it in 	the Belmont; 	he had a 	big nose
strip on his nose like the ones that you buy in the drug store 	– 	that 	you stick on
and they expand your nostrils so you don’t snore during the night 	.
The horse raced with a big ass version of this stuck on his nose and these 	things
have come and gone in racing at least three times; about 	five years ago they
made the rounds through harness 	racing and everybody had them.
You’d be in 	a 	race driving and these things are flying off the horses’ 	noses and
hitting you in the head as 	they 	went 	on 	by 	– 	just 	like they come off you in the
middle of the night.
Of course this is all nonsense. 	Horses’ 	breathing systems 	are entirely different
than human breathing systems. 	This does nothing; 	you might as well tie a lucky
shamrock to his ass.
Because that’s what this is. 	Superstition is what this is. But 	there’s a scientific
explanation…
So 	there’s science, there’s 	authority 	– 	borrowed authority, 	fake authority 	–
there’s celebrity.
Social, 	peer proof, one-on-one or in aggregate, 	is 	still 	usually the best.
Demonstration is also extremely powerful and something that 	many of us 	often
leave out that we don’t need to leave out. 	So we prove our case.
Mechanical Process
The 	mechanical process here by the way is 	how 	to make a list of every assertion.
So, 	if you’re working backwards, 	take your script, 	take your PowerPoint slides
or 	take your presentation that you’re already using.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 19 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	20
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
Either you or somebody capable goes through it and makes a list of every
assertion. You 	may be able to go back and cross some 	out 	that you are convinced
are 	common knowledge.
So, if your 	product 	in 	part works because there’s gravity, 	you ought to have it on
the list. Now you may ultimately cross that one out.
However if your product works 	because of evolution, 	the statistics tell us you
might want to have to deal with that one.
There was a thing in the news not too long ago about the number of people in
Congress who do not believe in evolution. 	I mean it’s a significant number.
In 	the last election, 	I think on the 	Republican side, 	it was all of the candidates in
the primary except for two.
If you happen to think that’s an 	accepted fact 	– 	and 	somehow your pitch is based
on everybody accepting the fact 	– 	you’re wrong.
So 	you make a list of 	every assertion and then you consider, “Do we have to
prove it?” 	You better then prove every 	one that you are going to make.
That’s the technique; 	that’s how you do that.
Human Interest Stories
In the 	Middle, 	we present our human interest stories.
So 	if you 	– 	or 	whoever it is that you’re building this for who is pitching 	– 	have 	an
origin story, 	a reason 	why 	story, 	a turning point in life story, 	we put that in the
Middle.
Great 	before and afters 	go in the 	Middle. 	The “Be-like-me” 	appeal goes in the
Middle.
The 	superstar testimonial, not all testimonials are equal.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 20 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	21
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
Most 	good presentations use a number of testimonials 	but you
may want to feature one or two more than you feature the others.
Sometimes that may be somebody with a severe handicap 	who 	was able to
succeed at whatever it is that you’re pitching, or use whatever it is that you are
selling.
In 	“Get Rich 	in Real 	Estate,” 	if we can find somebody with no legs and no arms,
who does the contracts by the pencil held in his 	teeth and makes a million
dollars a year, I would rather use that person than somebody with two legs and
two arms.
Because people get the idea, “Well a legless, 	armless guy can do it. 	I guess I don’t
have much of an excuse, because I could actually walk around and I can text
people.”
You’re looking for the poster boy or girl kind of testimonial to accentuate during
the course of 	a presentation.
You may be 	familiar with 	the Nancy
Kwan 	Pearl 	Cream 	spot 	but there
was also a Nancy Kwan 	infomercial
for an 	Oriental Beauty Secret 	skin
care system 	– 	a 	whole box of glop
rather than one thing.
Stan 	Jacobs 	and 	I 	spent 	a 	week
finding 	the 	testimonials 	and 	then
another 	week 	taping 	the
testimonials, 	and 	we 	found 	three
generations 	of women 	all using the
glop.
In the Middle, We Present
Human Interest Stories
 	Yours 	– 	the seller’s
o 	Origin/Reason Why/Turning
Point
o 	Before/After
o 	Be-Like-Me
 	The ‘Star’ Testimonial
Answers to & Invalidation of Doubts &
Objections
 	Known in advance
 	If ‘live’, as they happen
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 21 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	22
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
We found them 	in West Virginia I think; 	so there’s a grandma, 	there’s a mom
and there’s a daughter and they’re using the glop.
It’s a 	great human interest story, 	terrific. They go on the list to fly them in and
we go to interview them and of course we start with 	grandma.
Grandma is 209 years old and she’s shrunken, 	sort of looks like a Dr. Ruth kind
of character. She’s got jowls, I 	mean she’s got layers of skin like a 	big 	old 	Beetle
dog.
We’re going to show her now in a skin care commercial; 	Stan and I were going,
“I don’t know.” Mama looked okay and the kid looked fine 	but grandma…
Ultimately we decided to do it anyway because it was such a great human
interest story, 	“I’m on this product, 	then I got my daughter on this product and
her daughter is on this product.”
It’s better than, “I’m on this product and look how great I look.” This 	actually
was a more interesting presentation.
Just as an aside, 	the old woman is telling me 	– 	because 	they’re all 	on continuity,
the stuff is coming every month, the credit card is getting whacked 	– 	and 	she
says, 	“You know, 	my whole basement is almost full of this now 	and 	I give it away
to friends. I use less and less of it 	now 	because 	my 	face is getting smaller and I
don’t go out 	that much.”
It’s 	all 	I can do 	not 	to say, “Hun you can 	call up and stop.” 	But my client is the
one shipping it.
So you’re looking for the star testimonial and then you want to give them a little
more time in your presentation, than democratizing it.
You 	may want them with you live and the other testimonials just shown on tape.
You may want to give a whole segment in multi segments to that one testimonial
that is either on set with you live, that is in the room with you live or, 	for their
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 22 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	23
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
video, 	you take a 	really good crew and you go and you tape them in 	their natural
habitat 	with their dogs and kids and boats and houses.
Everybody else gets taped in a hotel room up against 	a 	background with 	a
potted 	palm and a bookcase. You make undemocratic decisions about this
including 	in 	the amount of time.
In 	the 	Middle, 	we 	also 	answer and 	invalidate the doubts and objections.
Time Frame
All this then falls into some sort of structure for the time frame 	– 	are 	you 	going
to have 	two hours on stage, 	are 	you 	going to have 	90 minutes on stage, are you
going to have 	45 minutes on stage?
Are they going to watch two hours, 	are they going to watch an hour, 	are they
going to watch a half?
I will almost always divide the 	Middle into 	either three or four, 	depending upon
the length of the 	Middle. 	So there are either three or four defined segments that
then these pieces get fit into.
There’s timing for this. For our webcasts, we immediately knew 	these are
structured, 	50/10-12; 	50/10–12, 50/10–12; the 28½ 	minute 	infomercial is
usually structured 	long, 	medium, 	short commercials in between but sometimes
others.
Special Middle Elements
There are 	special 	Middle elements. One is, 	as I just said, 	is there anybody that
there is a reason for you to have video of them 	in their habitat?
Most people shoot all 	the 	testimonials 	at 	one time, 	and 	they shoot them all in
one place. Often they don’t even bother to move around to four different
locations in the hotel room that they might be able to use. 	They don’t build sets
for 	them.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 23 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	24
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
So 	now, 	in your presentation, 	when we see the testimonials, 	it’s kind of evident
they were all shot at one time in one place.
Some people are 	less 	uptight in their natural environment.
One 	way some people cheat when they shoot them all in one place 	is 	they come
real tight so there’s no background at all. 	However, 	particularly in HD, a 	lot of
people don’t look so good real tight.
It 	definitely doesn’t work for skin care testimonials, it’s just not a good thing.
You have to think about whether there’s a reason and 	it 	is 	worth 	the money to
go get this piece of proof that we’re going to use in our presentations in 	their
world, 	rather than in a studio with a green screen or in a hotel room outside a
seminar.
So that’s one 	Middle element to think about is whether or not you’re going to
pick one or more to present in a more interesting way than just what they are
saying.
Because, 	if you back up now 	and 	you think about each isolated thing in a group
presentation… 	so if I was selling and I am now going to cut to showing you three
video testimonials, 	one right after the other, we’re back to the 	Open of 	a 	thing.
And 	when I 	open, 	if it is really not compelling, 	you will mentally tune out.
It’s more compelling if I open with my testimonial climbing out of his airplane or
standing on his boat 	holding the giant fish he just caught, 	than 	if I have him
sitting on a stool up against a
yellow wall with a 	palm tree
next to him.
Now 	it’s 	much 	more
cumbersome to go shoot 	him 	on
his boat 	with the fish; it’s a pain
in the ass 	because he will drop
Some Special Middle Elements
 	In Their Habitat
 	‘Unique’ Physical 	Locations
 	‘The Garden Party’ Chat-Fest
 	The ‘Man On The Street’
 	Live Audience, Call-In/Fake Call-In, Live
Online Chat & Q/A
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 24 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	25
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
the fish, 	the fish will bite him, 	the boat will rock and the camera will fall over
etc., 	but it could be worth it.
Unique physical locations. 	So 	of all the Dave Del Dotto 	“Get Rich in Real
Estate” 	infomercials 	– 	you 	can go back and see them all if you like; they’re
probably all on YouTube if that happens to interest you 	– 	he 	shot three with
John Davidson as the host.
If 	you don’t know who John Davidson is, Google him. At 	the time, 	most people
knew who John Davidson was.
The 	most successful show was a panel of 	Dave’s superstar testimonials on high
stools on the beach in Hawaii, mostly in Hawaiian 	shirts with the ocean behind
them at a hotel, 	with John Davison interviewing them on the beach.
It 	didn’t 	really 	have anything to do with anything 	but, when you clicked 	into it, it
was more visually interesting than in a four-walled room. So unique 	physical
locations, 	there 	is 	sometimes 	a 	reason for those.
Then we have the 	garden party scene. You 	don’t see a lot in web video but you
see it in almost every infomercial that has been successful, particularly those
aimed at women consumers. 	But you’ll see it in men’s shows too.
We 	call it the garden party scene. Sometimes it’s out… 	in 	Proactiv® 	and Victoria
Principal, 	there’s a few times where it is actually 	out on a patio in the garden.
More often than not, you 	will see this in the living room but the scene has four
or five or six 	– 	the 	garden party scene in the Vic show has 20 I think, because
they’re sitting in round tables 	on the patio.
If it’s in the living room, 	you’re kind of bound by a couch and a few chairs
without 	it looking like you’re not in a 	real 	living room.
There will be four or five; 	the celebrity will be there, 	the host will be there 	– 	if
there’s a host different than the celebrity 	– 	and they’re all sitting around
chatting about the product and their use 	of it and 	the benefits.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 25 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	26
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
It’s 	a loose, 	people talk over each other, 	back and forth kind of conversation. The
reason you see it more in shows 	aimed at 	women is because guys don’t do this.
You 	put guys in a living room 	and in 	five hours 	there’s 	like four words spoken 	–
and three of those you can’t put on television. 	So 	you don’t see it as much in
guys’ 	shows, but you see it a lot in women’s 	shows.
Now to get the four-minute clip that you might use in 	your infomercial, 	in your
webcast, 	in your DVD, 	however you’re going to use it… 	if I was going to cut to 	it
in part of a live presentation… 	you may shoot three hours and you’re going to
have 	some 	post 	because you really do want them to forget that they are 	doing
anything but 	chatting.
It’s 	an extremely powerful device because the viewer sort of steps into the room
with them and they have a conversational 	experience that doesn’t feel like it was
scripted and structured to advance the sale 	– 	which it wasn’t, 	that was done in
post-production 	in 	the way you edited it.
So 	it’s a very powerful tool, 	the garden party scene.
The 	man on the street 	deal. The 	man on the street 	again 	is used all the time; 	it’s
been used throughout history. 	Joe 	Sugarman’s BluBlocker 	show, 	the first three
successful shows were all man on the street 	– 	“Try 	the glasses on, 	what do you
think?” 	stopping 	people on the street.
A 	more 	recent 	orthotics 	infomercial for things you put in your shoe, 	they took
the truck 	to the shopping mall 	and they’re stopping people on the street and
they’re putting 	the 	thing in their shoe and they’re all reacting. There’s 	a 	fitness
show that was done that way 	– 	I 	think it was one of the 	“Body by Jake” 	shows,
I’m not sure.
You see it done a lot 	– 	some apparently spontaneous involvement of the public
in a verifying demonstration.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 26 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	27
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
Again the infomercial industry lives and lived on this, people doing video for the
web less, 	people doing video clips 	to show in live presentation less, 	but no good
reason. 	It works, it 	works, it works.
So 	you’ll see the 	“man on the street” 	stuff all the time. 	Again, if 	you use YouTube,
I assure you, you can find any of these infomercial examples and it is about a
verifying demonstration.
In 	Pitch 	Men 	– 	if 	you 	haven’t 	seen this show by 	the way, this predates Shark
Tank; you can buy it on DVD, it was about people coming to them with products
and them finally picking something and doing it. They 	did a 	“man on 	the 	street”
in their spots where they went to a fire house so they got firemen to 	try this
insole, 	this foot thing and then they interview the fire guys.
Because firemen are on their feet all the time and firemen fight fires and firemen
are very popular and firemen are trustworthy figures.
Now you might say why didn’t they go get a room full of podiatrists 	to do it?
They would be more credible authority 	figures 	for a foot insole than a bunch of
fire guys. 	Well think about it. 	How 	interested is anybody in podiatrists?
“Hey for the next three minutes, 	I’m going to show you a 	room full of
podiatrists.” Great, terrific you could 	have 	picked 	accountants.
We’re going to see a room full of fire fighter guys; that’s inherently more
exciting and interesting, so if you’re going to choose 	between the two, 	you want
fire fighters.
There’s the 	live 	audience 	thing 	– 	some 	version of a live response from an
audience. Again tons of infomercials are done this way 	– 	have 	been, 	still are. In
some cases, 	it’s a real audience, some cases it’s a rented audience.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 27 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	28
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
If you shoot in LA, 	you can spec them. 	There’s 	companies that 	just 	deliver you
the audience 	– 	you 	build 	a 	seating 	chart and you want a fat black woman right
there and you want an Asian guy right there and 	you want 	a 	much better looking
but older guy 	than you 	right there; 	you 	want a married couple here; 	they go get
them and put them in place and you get exactly what you spec.
With 	the live audience, 	the key is that they are
actually involved not just sitting 	there.
At some point, 	they are asking questions and
getting answers. Five 	of them 	have 	been picked
and 	coming 	up 	and 	participating 	in 	a
demonstration.
So 	all that’s 	in the 	Middle.
Holding Attention
Because 	the average American now has the attention span
of a ferret 	after three hours 	at Starbucks, we have to 	work 	at
holding their interest for long presentations.
The first thing you know about holding interest in long presentations 	in 	a 	short
attention span 	world is 	– 	get 	the 	best possible audience 	you can.
This 	is consistently 	the 	part of selling 	One to Many 	that so many people forget is
that the effectiveness even of the most effective presenter, 	in media or in
person, 	is heavily influenced by things that presenter can’t control by his
presenting 	and that the presentation can’t affect.
So 	you could put Tony in such a bad place that he could not be effective.
You can put the best one with the best presentation in front 	of a really bad
audience, and you will have low effectiveness.
With the live
audience, the key is
that 	they are
actually involved not
just sitting there.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 28 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	29
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
There is not a professional speaker in the country alive or dead who hasn’t
bombed 	– 	sometimes it’s obvious why, 	other times… for example I can’t sell
anything in Tucson.
I don’t know why that is but I
have established with sufficient
number 	of 	attempts, 	that 	I
cannot make money in Tucson,
Arizona. 	I just can’t do it.
No matter what I do in Tucson,
everybody 	walks 	out 	of 	the
room and nobody buys. So, 	after
six or eight of these 	experiences
over a decade, 	I haven’t been
back to Tucson in 30 years.
I have 	no interest in going there
and 	I 	would never go there on
vacation and give the bastards
money 	because 	they 	will 	not
give me any money. Tucson is out, 	so I have no explanation for that.
I can tell you why I can’t do well in Vermont; 	that I have an answer for you for 	–
because it’s Ben and 	Jerry land. Even if I don’t say anything, 	they sense from me
that we profoundly disagree over pretty much everything in life.
I flew from Phoenix to Seattle for a Peter Lowe 	gig with either Ben 	or 	Jerry
sitting next to me; I get them confused, 	I don’t remember which one.
The flight got cancelled three times and they had to bring 	in 	additional planes,
so we were 	at 	Sky 	Harbor Airport 	in Phoenix 	for a while before we even got on a
plane.
How to Hold Interest in Long & Even Very Long
Presentations in a Short Attention Span World
1. 	Best possible audience + dispense praise
for the smart/committed ones and
shame on the dumb/lazy 	ones
2. 	Most relevant message possible
3. 	Organization into 3-Act or 4-Act Plays
with segments within each Act
4. 	Periodic cliffhangers, teases and new
headlines
5. 	Mixed content: information, humor,
story, demo, etc.
6. 	Mixed media: photos, cartoons, video,
text
7. 	Enough 	speed and pace they must focus
to keep up
8. 	Momentum 	– 	pace picking up during the
presentation
9. 	Possibly break the presentation up into
a series of presentations
10. 	Consider lying to them about elapsed
and coming amounts of time
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 29 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	30
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
We conversed 	for 	four hours, 	four and a half hours… 	we’re landing in 	Seattle
and, 	whichever one of them 	it 	was, 	Ben 	or 	Jerry, says to me, 	“I haven’t asked you,
do you like ice cream?”
I said, “Yeah, 	everybody likes 	ice 	cream.” He says, 	“Great, 	we finally found the
one thing in four and a half hours about which we have any agreement!”
So, 	sometimes there is a reason 	and 	sometimes you don’t know the reason 	but
who 	you 	put this presenter and presentation in front of matters a lot, and you’ve
got to give thought to who you invest money or time in driving to see it.
The worst thing about infomercials is you really can’t target audience much; it’s
the toughest thing about that. Whereas, 	on the web, 	you can pick and choose
who 	you 	drive to go watch it.
When you put 	them 	in a room you can pick and choose.
For 	four years, 	we did preview 	seminars for chiropractors and dentists 	mostly. 	I
had one speaker on the road three weeks out of
every four. For a couple of 	the 	years I went out
for one week out the month; 	we’re putting docs
in the room three hours a night and we’re selling
them 	stuff.
We learned as we went along. 	First 	of all, 	there
was some geography we never went back to; 	you did it once and you never went
there again because, 	compared to all the other geographies, 	the response is
horrible.
Downtown New York, 	Manhattan 	is 	very tough to make work 	– 	I 	think in four
and a half years 	we did it twice 	– 	even 	though you’ve 	got great density and
you’ve 	got 	all that.
Who 	you put this
presenter and
presentation in front
of matters a lot.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 30 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	31
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
The 	one I did I remember vividly. The doc comes in, 	he’s got four staff people
with him; 	the four staff people 	are 	in the back, 	he sits right in front, 	he puts his
feet up on 	the table and he reads a newspaper.
I’m not selling him. 	He 	has come predetermined that he’s going to verify his
conclusion which is, “It’s 	all bullshit and it won’t work in New York,” 	and he’s
going to go home.
We also, 	over 	time, 	quickly learned that women 	doctors with 	hyphenated names
didn’t buy, we didn’t do very well with Asians 	anywhere except 	in California.
So you stop mailing them and gradually we were putting more and more in the
rooms who were well matched with the message and the presenter, and 	fewer
and fewer who weren’t matched.
By the end of six months with Foster on the road, 	we were at a 	95% close rate
every night. 	Some 	of it was him because he was really good but some of it 	was
what we did about, 	if 	you 	will, 	rigging the audience in front 	of him.
You have to think about rigging the audience 	and, from there, 	links 	the 	most
relevant message possible.
If 	I take the generic, 	great unwashed 	masses 	VSL script for 	“Get Rich in Real
Estate” 	and I drive the 	Investor’s 	Business 	Daily 	crowd to it, and I 	drive 	Home
Service business owners 	– 	carpet cleaning, pest
control, gutter repair 	– 	and 	I drive them to the
same VSL, 	yes some of each will buy; 	but 	not
nearly 	as 	many 	as 	if 	I 	drive 	the 	Investor’s
Business Daily crowd 	to one 	Video Sales 	Letter
and I drive the 	Home 	Service guys to another one
– 	because I can make the message relevant to
them.
It’s not five times
harder 	to get five
different letters 	but
you’ll get much more
effectiveness.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 31 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	32
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
And 	why wouldn’t you? 	How 	hard is it to do five 	instead of one? 	It’s not five
times harder but you’ll 	get much more effectiveness. 	So the most relevant
message possible.
You have to hold interest so another 	thing to think about it is not one long
continuous presentation with a pitch at the end.
In many 	cases, 	you can get away with that in a physical room, particularly if they
have come there to be with you or they have paid money to be with you. You can
use a 	“one act play” 	structure 	– 	we 	start, 	we go, 	we pitch, 	we finish.
But, 	in almost any other media, 	it’s very hard to hold people’s interest.
Our presentation in the Success 	Track years was three hours long; 	so we put
them in a room 	and 	we held them 	for three hours.
When you sent that to them on a CD and on a DVD, 	it didn’t work very well
because they 	don’t have to stay.
Now it had to be cut up into pieces with the pitch in there more than once, 	just
like we now do on the live webcasts.
If you did the webcast for four hours like you do when in the room, 	Dave, 	I, Lee,
whoever 	else is on camera, 	we would all start, we 	would go for three hours and
40 minutes and then we would pitch. That 	would not work nearly as well as we
might be able to get away with it in a room.
So 	most of these presentations get divided into 	three-act or four-act plays.
The first 	act is mostly the 	Open, 	the second or the third act is 	Middle, 	the fourth
act is 	End but there are 	buying opportunities at least between the acts 	if not
during the acts.
In 	the 	infomercial world, 	all three cover the territory needed to make a buying
decision because they click in and out.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 32 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	33
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
Periodic cliffhangers, 	teases 	and 	new headlines. 	Whenever there’s a shift
from one act to the other, when there is a 	“go to commercial” 	and 	“we’re coming
back from commercial,” 	when we know from statistics that we lose a lot 18
minutes in, 	at these points you now need cliffhangers and teases.
You’ve 	got to go back and write three promises of what’s to come; 	you’ve 	got to
tell people what’s going to happen next, 	even as you are telling them something
currently 	– 	so 	that you hold onto them for a few more minutes.
In the infomercial world again we grow up thinking about, 	“One more minute;
one more minute.” 	What 	do we have to do to hold this person for one more
minute?
Hal 	had a show one 	year 	that just sort of sagged right around 15, 	16 minutes in
and 	you knew people were bailing out of this left and right.
Hal 	did cheap shows, 	he did fast shows, 	he didn’t 	do a lot of money in post and I
remember it was 	a 	subliminal tape pitch and the presenter was pretty good.
He wanted to 	do something really cheap to fix it so he had two girls in bikinis
just walk across the set 	behind the guy 	real slow. 	They 	walked on, 	they walked
across the set 	as 	they walked off.
Nobody said anything about them; 	they had nothing to do with the show,
nobody referenced anything. But 	when you were sitting there watching it, 	you
were 	about ready to… and then 	you 	stayed for another couple of minutes.
I’m not sure I recommend the technique but I am suggesting 	to 	you 	there is a
time and place.
Mixed 	content presentations. 	It’s very hard 	to 	sell with an information
presentation 	only. It’s even hard just to hold people’s attention who’ve 	paid for
an information presentation only; 	very hard.
You 	need a mix 	– 	you need stories, 	you need humor, 	you need demonstration;
you need something going on.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 33 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	34
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
The 	joke about movies 	– 	Carla will 	occasionally want me to go to some chick
flick thing; 	I’ll say, “Is there a car 	crash in the first 15 minutes?” “No, 	I don’t think
so.” “Fine, 	you go.”
I mean I want something to happen; I don’t want a two-hour meaning of life
conversation. That is why we got divorced in the first place 	– 	this 	two-hour
meaning of life conversation. Let’s not go 	watch people have a two-hour
meaning of life conversation.
Mixed 	media 	– 	photos, cartoons, video, text. In our 	“Get Rich 	in Real 	Estate”
stuff, 	we get away with a lot 	– 	big 	lengths 	of 	PowerPoint 	and voice, 	really a sales
letter stuck up on the web.
But the longer they get 	– 	the 	more we need 	to do 	longer ones 	– 	the 	more we are
using cartoons and graphics and video and changing type fonts 	and all the stuff
you would do if you could in 	print.
There has to be 	enough speed and pace 	that
they have to work just 	a little 	at keeping up. It
can’t be a steady pace all the way through. Pace
matters. 	You 	can’t be leaving them behind but
they can’t be ahead of you either.
You’ve all sat in 	live performances where that’s the case or watched a TV show
or a movie where that’s the case. You are two sentences 	ahead of them 	and you
now know what they’re going to say, you’re saying it in your head before…
So 	remember CSI: 	Miami 	and 	David Caruso? 	If 	you 	watched 	CSI: 	Miami, 	it’s
painful because 	I’m like eight 	sentences 	ahead of this guy. It’s 	the same pace if
he’s saying, “I need a cup of coffee” 	and he’s 	saying, “Oh there’s 	a third dead
body.” It’s 	the same pace, 	it never changed.
So 	pace matters, 	momentum 	matters. Most presentations need to build as they
go along.
Most presentations
need to build as they
go along.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 34 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	35
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
By the way, 	the worst pitch people ever 	– 	who 	can’t pitch well from the front 	–
they 	tend to do the opposite; 	the closer they get to the pitch, 	the slower and
more awkward and clumsy and uncomfortable they get. 	The 	good ones, 	the pace
builds 	during the course of a presentation.
You 	may want to 	break the presentation up 	into a series of presentations that
are complete. They have an open, 	they have a beginning, 	they have a middle,
they have a bridge 	to a close, 	they have close, they have some sort of commercial
and then they do all that again and sometimes they do it all again. Many
infomercials are structured that way.
By 	all means 	lie to them about 	elapsed 	time 	and amount of time coming.
Either 	from the stage or in 	canned 	presentations, 	I have never told the truth
about this in my entire life.
I always say, “In 	four minutes,” 	and it’s nine; “In just a few more minutes,” 	and
it’s 	20. 	“17 minutes 	from 	now, 	you’re going to see,” and it’s 	22.
They 	have a sense of time but they’re not clocking you
and you want to stretch them.
If you tell them the truth, 	they start to think about 	baling 	because 22 minutes is
a lot 	of 	time; 	14 minutes, 	that’s not so much time. 	So by 	all means 	lie.
7 Things They 	Must Realize 	Before They Will Buy
Generally now, 	we’ve 	talked about sequence of agreements; 	there’s seven things
that you want in every group presentation 	– 	they 	have to agree to these things;
they have to realize these things, 	they have to accept these things in order to
buy from you.
One 	is 	appropriateness 	– 	whatever 	it 	is 	that 	you’re 	selling 	has 	to 	be
appropriate for that. Hence the presentation to the mail box shop owners 	that
we talked about earlier 	fails this and nothing else you’re going to do will 	save
that.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 35 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	36
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
The authority, 	the credibility, 	the 	offer, 	the guarantee 	– 	nothing 	else is going to
save it if it is judged completely inappropriate for me; 	I’m out.
Differentiation 	– 	somehow 	what you’re offering has to be ideally different from
every prior attempt they’ve made at getting the same outcome.
Rarely are we selling anything to anybody who hasn’t had a prior attempt at
getting the same outcome.
The 	arthritis pitch 	– 	everybody has 	got glop for pain relief.
Whoever 	is going to buy glop from me this week for pain relief 	has got a 	drawer
full of glop that’s all been half-used 	and 	left in the drawer 	and they’ve thrown
out another drawer 	full of glop.
They’ve tried a lot of glop; 	so somehow they’ve 	got to be convinced there’s a
difference.
Your 	buyers are Charlie Brown
trying to 	kick the football 	Lucy
holds. 	They’ve 	had 	it 	pulled
away 	from 	them 	and 	they’ve
fallen 	on 	their 	ass 	again 	and
again 	and again.
We 	can make 	up for it most
when we 	are 	selling 	them one
on one; 	we can make up for it
next most when we 	are in a
room. 	Now, 	when you start to
get distance, 	it’s harder and harder. 	So 	you’ve 	got to convince them it’s different.
If 	you have to settle for the proverbial 	“New and 	Improved,” 	you at least do that
but ideally it’s different.
7 Things They Must Realize & Know 	As A
Result Of Your Presentation Before They
Will Buy From You
1. 	Appropriateness
2. 	Differentiation
3. 	Excitement
4. 	They Are NOT The Only Idiot
5. 	They’re Getting “A Deal”
6. 	They Won’t Fail, Won’t Be
Embarrassed or Frustrated, Won’t
Be “Disappointed Again” And Can’t
Lose
7. 	There Are Reasons They MUST Hurry
or Do It Now.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 36 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	37
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
There has to be some 	excitement; 	whatever it is you’re selling them has to
somehow be cool or exciting to own, or fun 	and they have to get that from your
presentation.
If 	you watch the 	“pocket hose” 	thing, 	the cool factor is 	that, 	by itself, 	it 	comes
back to you. 	It 	coils itself back up, 	that’s really cool.
You kind of 	want to 	own one just to watch it do that. Now that’s 	not the best
reason to have 	it 	but that’s its cool factor.
So 	ideally, 	there’s some cool factor to what it is that you’re selling. When you’re
doing visual selling, 	the reason to do visual selling is to show something. 	So, 	if
you don’t take advantage of that 	– 	and 	you don’t show things…
In 	many cases 	– 	and 	I actually feel this somewhat 	about some of what 	we do 	–
you go 	to 	all the trouble to do the online video launch and the five videos and
the talking heads for four hours.
The thought that always occurs to me 	is, if 	you just gave the same number of
people who tuned 	in to watch this and let me send them a sales letter, we could
skip all this and probably get the same damn results because we don’t really
have anything to show.
The purpose of video is to show stuff 	– 	that’s its purpose.
I had a client for some years in Beverly Hills 	– 	a 	cosmetic 	surgeon 	– 	and 	he
would bring people into evening seminars to book surgery. He discovered pretty
quick that, 	if 	you had 	five or six live people there whose breasts 	you had 	fixed
and made terrific, then when you show the 	“before” 	picture and then they
opened 	their blouses and let everybody see it, 	and you can walk up and touch it
– 	which 	women do by the way 	– 	this was much 	more powerful.
It would even 	be powerful on video 	– 	instead of him just standing there talking
and showing slides. 	Visual presentations 	are 	supposed to be visual.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 37 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	38
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
They 	have to know 	they’re not the only idiot. A 	big problem with selling 	One to
Many 	when nobody sees the many….
If we’re selling 	One to Many 	here, 	you see the many; so 	you start when you come
into a room.
It’s in part 	why Pittsburg 	went 	better than Cleveland for Bill 	because he had 	five
times more people at the Pittsburg seminar than he had at 	the Cleveland
seminar.
The 	room is flatter, 	people don’t like to laugh when there’s not that many people
in the room. Most importantly, 	they come in the room and they think, “I’m one of
a few idiots.”
If 	they come in a room and there’s a bunch more people, 	they say to themselves,
“They can’t all be idiots.”
When we do 	One to Many 	physically, 	this factor can take care of itself.
For Geier more recently and when I did the Success Track, 	if we had 35, 	40, 	50
docs 	in the room in San Jose on a Tuesday night, 	that was enough. Every doc 	felt
pretty good. He might know somebody but at least here’s 	33, 	34, 	35 peers.
It 	validates the idea that I came, that this is an 	okay place to be.
If 	he 	walked 	in and saw six, 	the sales job suddenly gets infinitely harder because
he thinks, “I’m one of six 	morons, 	Uh-oh!”
Well, 	if you’re doing 	One to Many 	but they’re watching it on a screen, they’re the
only idiot and so you have to figure out a way to counter that visually as well as
what you say.
QVC home shopping for example figured 	out 	how to counter 	that 	by having
people call 	in and by showing the ticker … 200 	have 	sold, 	300 	have 	sold.
Of course you had no way of knowing if that’s legit, they can start it just like the
piano player puts some money in the jar before he starts for the night.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 38 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	39
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
You never start with an empty jar 	– 	bad 	idea. If 	you have a restaurant and it’s
new you’ve 	got to go rent some cars and put them in the parking lot.
With a chiropractor’s office, it’s the same; we 	teach them that all the time.
You’ve 	got to go rent 	eight, ten 	cars 	and 	you’ve 	got to move them around. So
nobody ever 	drives by this place and sees…
You 	want to see a really dumb 	chiropractic 	office 	– 	you’ll see a brand new office,
you’ll see a sign 	“New Office, Patients Accepted” and 	no cars in the parking lot.
Mary books an appointment; 	she comes down, 	she drives in the parking lot, 	she
drives off the parking lot. 	“Uh-oh!” 	she says to herself. It’s the 	same thing with a
restaurant; 	people drive in the parking lot, it’s 6:30 at night 	and 	there’s only two
cars here, “Whoops!” 	back out of the driveway
they go.
Same things happens with 	all this. Nobody wants
to be the only idiot. 	Nobody wants to be the only
one getting crippled for life; 	nobody wants to be
the only person getting food poisoning 	tonight.
You’ve 	got to put some money in the jar before you start
playing if you want to get 	money in the jar.
You’ve 	got to understand when you’re doing 	One to Many, 	but they’re getting it
one on a screen, 	that’s like coming into a room and being 	one and you sell to
them.
They have to believe 	they’re getting 	a 	deal 	– 	rich 	person, 	poor person 	– 	it 	don’t
make any difference. They’ve 	got to somehow believe they’re getting a great
deal.
If 	they’re watching it
on a screen, they’re
the only idiot so you
have to figure out a
way to counter that.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 39 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	40
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
They have to 	be convinced that 	they’re not going to fail; 	they’re not going to be
embarrassed; 	they’re 	not going to be frustrated; they’re not going to 	be
disappointed again.
Finally, 	they’ve 	got to believe in 	reasons to do
this now. Again, 	you’re handicapped when they
can’t see people doing it now 	– 	which is why the
QVC thing works.
So, 	in the physical environment, if you know how
to 	start 	a 	stampede 	you 	can 	usually 	start 	a
stampede.
If you don’t know how to start a stampede 	– 	and 	you don’t have an ethical issue
with it 	– 	you can use shills 	(fake customers) 	to start a stampede.
You 	can just 	seed 	the audience with 	8, 	10, 	12 people who travel with you from
city to 	city 	to city and 	their job is to buy.
They sit there, 	they take notes and they’re the first ones who jump up and run to
the back of the room very excitedly. Don’t think people don’t do it.
You can create quasi shills so you can make sure you’ve 	got really good
customers in the room 	who buy 	everything 	along with the prospects. You can
coupon some people in advance.
There’s 	things you can do if you’re not particularly good at creating a stampede;
but, however you 	get it done, 	it does feed on itself.
As soon as some people start moving to buy, 	other people buy.
At 	the state fair or 	county fair, 	in 	the 	tent, 	the guy 	is 	still doing the knives. 	As
soon as one or 	two people buy, 	people 	who were standing way at the back
during the demo because they didn’t want to get called on and they didn’t want
to get pressed to buy, they start moving forward and buying.
You 	can make sure
you’ve got really
good customers in
the room who buy
everything.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 40 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	41
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
Last time when I was in Disney for the 	Food 	Show 	– 	during 	Carla’s 	free class that
cost me a fortune 	– 	before I bought all 	the balls I never actually even got out of
the 	Expo 	hall.
The Cutco knife 	guy was there, and doing the demo and they all know by the
way how to cut that 	tomato, 	it’s a skill. My guess is they 	could do it with their
thumb, 	I don’t think it has anything to do with the knife.
So 	the demo, 	he 	has a 	conversation 	with you and talks to you; it 	probably takes
20 minutes for him to do 	everything. While he is doing that to me 	– 	I’ve
predetermined 	I’m going to buy by the way 	– 	people 	stop and they’re behind
and around me, 	kind of watching but 	not wanting to be reached out and
grabbed.
So 	I’m a good enthusiastic buyer. I’m buying excitedly and I’m buying two sets,
they were like 	$800 I think, so I got 	$1600 worth of knives. 	Carla 	didn’t see
those, 	they ship 	them. All she saw was a pile of $200 balls; 	that was bad enough!
But 	the people who had gathered around, 	they all bought 	– 	the guy got
everybody.
After they all leave, 	I said, “You should give
me those for free.” He said, “You’re absolutely
right. The 	attitude of the first one to buy
controls 	the 	closing 	percentage 	of 	the
percentage that are there to buy.”
We can make that happen in a physical environment.
Now 	Charlie Brown is watching you on a screen; 	he’s home alone, 	he doesn’t see
it. 	So it’s good for us to make sure he knows 	that 	it’s happening.
QVC does it with the clicker 	– 	there’s only 	so many left, 	there’s been so many
sold. 	If 	you watch QVC at all, 	you will notice every single time 	that 	there’s
The attitude of the
first one to buy
controls the closing
percentage.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 41 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	42
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
momentum; 	you’ll never see something on QVC where there’s a big rush of sales
when they first start and then it trickles down.
It doesn’t happen; 	it’s always the other way, 	there’s a momentum.
We 	do it with webcasts 	by, 	every time we come back from a break, recognizing
Bill Brown 	from 	Tampa 	and Suzie from the UK and somebody with some
unpronounceable name with all vowels in it from somewhere.
We do the romper room, 	“Barbara is 	with us this morning and Charlie is with us
this morning.”
We’re not doing that to give 	a 	pat on the head to the buyer, 	we’re doing it as
demonstration that people are buying.
So you’ve 	got to think about this issue.
Mandatory Explanation
Now, still in the Middle, 	the big one 	– 	the 	mandatory explanation, 	the question
you’ve 	got to answer that people care deeply about, 	that has to be taken care of
in the 	Middle before they can buy 	from you 	that nobody should care about at all,
is 	why you are selling to them other than to make money.
Nobody should ever care about it 	– 	the 	knives are either good or bad. Obviously
the guy is there to make money because he rented space and he’s standing there
and he’s selling knives.
He can’t deny he’s there to make money but
people deeply care for no good reason.
If 	I need heart surgery, 	I should want the
best heart surgeon in the world. 	If I 	go into
his office for the consult and there’s money
on the wall, 	and there’s gold dollar signs,
and there is a pirate flag, 	I shouldn’t give a
shit.
The One MANDATORY
Explanation & Answer 	to
THE Question Consumers
Should NOT Care One
Whit About, Yet
Desperately DO Care.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 42 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	43
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
What 	do I care? 	I only care I got 	the 	best heart guy 	I can get.
But 	that’s not how people’s heads work; 	it just isn’t the way their heads work.
They want there to be a mission, 	a reason in addition to… 	other than the money.
They 	want you selling this health product because your 	sister died from this
condition. They want you in the fire alarm business because of some reason
other than they’re 	eight grand and I make three grand every time I sell a set.
Now again, 	they shouldn’t care; 	they should only care 	I’m 	getting good fire
alarms and now my dog won’t be the lone survivor. That’s all they should care
about, but people care about the other thing.
Somewhere in the middle of your presentation needs to
be a reason you’re doing what you’re doing that does not
directly have to do 	with the money you’re making.
If you go through my Peter Lowe 	presentation carefully and you listen to most
other 	times 	when 	I 	sell, 	if 	it’s 	generic, 	I 	will 	introduce 	the 	“Dan 	loves
entrepreneurs and 	small business people and I grew up with my parents as
small business people 	and we are the backbone of 	America.”
I will be the champion of the small business guy I am selling to.
If 	I’m selling to chiropractors, 	it’s because I love chiropractors, I’ve 	worked 	with
chiropractors my entire life. I’ve always had back trouble and if it wasn’t for
chiropractors I wouldn’t be standing here 	in front of 	you today.
They 	shouldn’t give a rat’s 	ass about that. If I’ve 	got a box of stuff that’s going to
flood their office with patients, it shouldn’t matter 	to 	them 	if I never go to a
chiropractor, 	I don’t like chiropractors, 	I wouldn’t let a chiropractor touch me
for love 	nor 	money and I’m an MD.
They shouldn’t care about any of that but they do.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 43 of 44 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	44
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 2
So 	it’s necessary to be there for the presentation to work to 	its fullest extent.
It’s helpful if you can find a way for 	this 	to be authentic. It’s helpful 	– 	but 	it is
essential for it to be there.
I have no advice for you about 	how to put it there if it’s not authentic. 	Hopefully
you’re smart enough to figure that out for yourself. 	But it’s necessary to be
there.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 44 of 44 --
